stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • The standard QWERTY keyboard layout was designed in the 1870s to prevent the keys of early typewriters from jamming.
  • Because common letter pairs are spaced apart, typists' fingers must travel longer distances on a QWERTY keyboard.
  • The Dvorak keyboard layout, developed as an alternative to QWERTY, places the most common letters on the home row.
  • Because typists' fingers stay on or near the home row, the Dvorak layout supports faster typing with less hand fatigue.
stem: The student wants to explain an advantage of the Dvorak keyboard. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: The QWERTY keyboard was designed to space common letter pairs apart, which is the opposite of the Dvorak layout's approach.
Designed so that the most common letters appear on the home row, the Dvorak layout supports faster typing with less hand fatigue than does the QWERTY layout.
Adopting the Dvorak layout would help solve a problem with the QWERTY layout.
The Dvorak keyboard layout was developed as an alternative to the QWERTY layout, which was designed in the 1870s.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. The sentence explains an advantage of the Dvorak keyboard, noting that it supports faster typing with less hand fatigue than does the QWERTY layout and describing how it does so (by placing the most common letters on the home row).

Choice A is incorrect. The sentence compares the layouts' approach to spacing common letters; it doesn't explain an advantage of the Dvorak keyboard. Choice C is incorrect. While the sentence refers to a possible reason to adopt the Dvorak layout, it doesn't identify the problem or the solution and thus doesn't explain the advantage of the Dvorak keyboard. Choice D is incorrect. The sentence describes the origins of the Dvorak layout; it doesn't explain an advantage of it.
